full_response:
Step 1.  Recall the terminology  
• A tensor of type (k , ℓ) carries k contravariant (upper) and ℓ covariant (lower) indices.  
• The rank (or valence) of a tensor is the total number of indices: k+ℓ.  
• A rank-1 (1,0) tensor is called a (contravariant) vector.  
• A rank-1 (0,1) tensor is called a covector or 1-form.

Step 2.  Examine each object  

1. A_a  
   • One lower index ⇒ type (0,1).  
   • Rank = 1.  
   • Because it is rank-1 with a lower index, it is a 1-form (covector), not a vector.

2. B^a  
   • One upper index ⇒ type (1,0).  
   • Rank = 1.  
   • Rank-1 with an upper index ⇒ a (contravariant) vector.

3. C_{ab}  
   • Two lower indices ⇒ type (0,2).  
   • Rank = 2.  
   • Rank-2 object; neither a vector nor a 1-form (it is a covariant rank-2 tensor).

\boxed{\begin{aligned}
\text{Rank}(A_a)&=1,\; \text{1-form (covector)}\\
\text{Rank}(B^a)&=1,\; \text{vector (contravariant)}\\
\text{Rank}(C_{ab})&=2,\; \text{neither vector nor 1-form (rank-2 covariant tensor)}
\end{aligned}}

Confidence: 92%
